target.name: Anduril Industries
target.founder: Palmer Luckey, Brian Schimpf, Trae Stephens, Matt Grimm, Joe Chen

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Defense & Security (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 23 same-sector peers exist in Defense & Security, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

data.narrative:
Anduril Industries is a Series H defense and security company founded by Palmer Luckey, Brian Schimpf, Trae Stephens, Matt Grimm, and Joe Chen, currently valued at $78B+ after raising $11B+. The relevant comp universe spans 23 same-sector peers, comprising 2 public companies (DRSHF, IDEAFORGE.NS) and 8 private firms including Saronic, Flock Safety, Shield AI, Chaos Industries, and Skydio.

Traditional peer-median valuation proves inapplicable due to insufficient comparable data points. No same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-tier peers meeting minimum thresholds (≥3 datapoints) exist within the narrow Defense & Security universe. Applying an all-sector median would introduce material distortion given the heterogeneity in capital intensity, regulatory environment, and growth profiles across defense subsegments. Consequently, triangulation methods requiring robust peer clustering cannot be reliably deployed here.

The $78B+ valuation rests on qualitative assessment of Anduril's market position, capital deployment ($11B+ raised), and strategic importance within U.S. defense autonomy, absent quantitative cross-validation through comparable multiples. Key uncertainties include the sustainability of current defense spending trajectories, competitive intensity from established primes and well-funded entrants, and execution risk at scale. Any valuation conclusion should acknowledge the absence of empirical peer anchoring and treat the mark as position-dependent rather than market-derived.
